summary:
The Ptolemaic dynasty (305–30 BC) made Alexandria, Egypt the intellectual capital of the Hellenistic world through deliberate royal patronage of scholarship. The Ptolemies founded or funded the Mouseion (the institution from which the word 'museum' derives) and the Great Library of Alexandria, which aimed to collect all the world's texts. Ptolemy I and II attracted leading scholars — Euclid, Archimedes (visiting), Eratosthenes, Callimachus, and Apollonius of Rhodes among them — transforming Alexandria into an unparalleled center of mathematics, astronomy, medicine, geography, and literary scholarship.

Alexandrian science under Ptolemaic patronage achieved lasting results: [[event:1198|Euclid's Elements]] systematized geometry; [[event:1199|Eratosthenes Measures Earth]] produced an accurate measurement of Earth's circumference; [[event:1200|Aristarchus Heliocentric Model]] proposed heliocentrism; and Herophilus and Erasistratus conducted the first systematic human dissections, founding scientific anatomy. The Library's collection was used for critical textual scholarship — editors like Aristarchus of Samothrace produced standardized texts of Homer. This concentration of learning in a single institution under royal support was unprecedented and briefly concentrated more of the ancient world's written knowledge in one place than had ever existed before.
